content.problemStatement:
You are given an empty grid with NNN rows (numbered 111 through NNN) and MMM columns (numbered 111 through MMM). You should fill this grid with integers in a way that satisfies the following rules:


For any three cells c1c_1c1‚Äã, c2c_2c2‚Äã and c3c_3c3‚Äã such that c1c_1c1‚Äã shares a side with c2c_2c2‚Äã and another side with c3c_3c3‚Äã, the integers written in cells c2c_2c2‚Äã and c3c_3c3‚Äã are distinct.
Let's denote the number of different integers in the grid by KKK; then, each of these integers should lie between 111 and KKK inclusive.
KKK should be minimum possible.
Find the minimum value of KKK and a resulting (filled) grid. If there are multiple solutions, you may find any one.
constraints: the sum of N‚ãÖMN \cdot MN‚ãÖM over all test cases does not exceed 7‚ãÖ1057 \cdot 10^57‚ãÖ105
content.inputFormat:
The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first and only line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and MMM.
content.outputFormat:
For each test case, print N+1N+1N+1 lines.
The first line should contain a single integer ‚Äî the minimum KKK.
Each of the following NNN lines should contain MMM space-separated integers between 111 and KKK inclusive. For each valid i,ji, ji,j, the jjj-th integer on the iii-th line should denote the number in the iii-th row and jjj-th column of the grid.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Example case 1: There is only one cell in the grid, so the only valid way to fill it is to write 111 in this cell. Note that we cannot use any other integer than 111.
Example case 2: For example, the integers written in the neighbours of cell (2,2)(2, 2)(2,2) are 111, 222 and 333; all these numbers are pairwise distinct and the integer written inside the cell (2,2)(2, 2)(2,2) does not matter. Note that there are pairs of neighbouring cells with the same integer written in them, but this is OK.
